paper.title: Genetics in Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Due to 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency and Clinical Implications

entities.name: Genetics in Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Due to 21-Hydroxylase Deficiency and Clinical Implications

evidence_entities.text: Of all congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), 95% to 99% is 21-hydroxylase deficiency (21OHD), an autosomal recessive disease.
21OHD is due to an insufficiency of 21-hydroxylase enzyme, which is encoded by the CYP21A2 gene and involved in cortisol and aldosterone production.
The predominant enzyme deficiency that accounts for 95% to 99% of all CAH cases is 21-hydroxylase deficiency (21OHD).
The 21OH enzyme converts 17α-hydroxyprogesterone to 11-deoxycortisol and progesterone to deoxycorticosterone.
The serum 17α-hydroxyprogesterone (17OHP) concentration is utilized as the principal marker for 21OHD.
CYP21A2 (OMIM*613815), encoding the steroid 21OH enzyme, and CYP21A1P pseudogene involve 10 exons spanning 3.4 kb.
Over 95% of deleterious variants leading to 21OHD are attributable to intergenic recombination events where microconversions represent 70% to 75%.
21OHD is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner. The milder of the 2 affected alleles will typically determine the phenotype.
The genetic cause of CAH-X syndrome is a recombination event between TNXB and its pseudogene TNXA.
The resulting phenotype is a hypermobility form of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS).

relationships.subject: stk19
relationships.note: These are adjacent genes in the RCCX complex, not the same entity

notes: This review paper provides comprehensive coverage of the genetics of 21-hydroxylase deficiency, including molecular mechanisms, diagnostic approaches, and clinical implications.
The RCCX complex structure and its role in disease pathogenesis is extensively detailed.
CAH-X syndrome represents a contiguous gene deletion syndrome affecting both CYP21A2 and TNXB genes.
